{
  "topic": "Advanced System Design Mastery",
  "topic_slug": "advanced-system-design-mastery",

  "goal": "Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.",

  "modules": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "title": "The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy",
      "estimated_minutes": 90,
      "description": "Before designing distributed systems, you must understand the immutable hardware constraints of a single machine. This module grounds you in the latency numbers that dictate all higher-level software design.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Mathematically quantify system constraints using hardware latency numbers and identify the primary bottleneck in any given system architecture.",
        "concepts": [
          "Latencies Every Programmer Should Know",
          "L1/L2/L3 Cache Mechanics",
          "Main Memory (RAM) access times",
          "Disk Seek and Network I/O latency",
          "The Bottleneck Principle (CPU vs Memory vs I/O bound)"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "Designing Data-Intensive Applications (DDIA)"
        ],
        "prerequisites": []
      },
      "subtopics": [],

      }
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "title": "Concurrency Primitives: Processes, Threads, and Coroutines",
      "estimated_minutes": 120,
      "description": "High-level abstractions often mask the immense cost of context switching. Here, you will unearth exactly how the OS kernel manages execution and why modern high-concurrency systems rely on user-space threads.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Explain kernel vs. user-space transitions and justify the use of thread pools and green threads in high-throughput environments.",
        "concepts": [
          "CPU Concurrency vs. Parallelism",
          "OS Kernel vs. User space transitions",
          "Context Switching Overhead",
          "OS Threads vs. User-space Threads (Goroutines/Green threads)",
          "Thread Pools and Resource Exhaustion"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [],

          "The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy"
        ]

    {
      "id": 3,
      "title": "Storage Hardware: Disk I/O Mechanics",
      "estimated_minutes": 90,
      "description": "Software design must bow to physical hardware limits. Understanding how SSDs and HDDs actually write data is crucial for understanding why certain databases exist.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Analyze the difference between random and sequential writes at the hardware level and explain how this impacts software data structures.",
        "concepts": [

      "id": 4,
      "title": "High-Performance I/O Models",
      "estimated_minutes": 120,
      "description": "This is the engine of servers like Nginx and Redis. We break down how an operating system can handle millions of concurrent connections without exploding thread counts.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Master the mechanics of non-blocking and asynchronous I/O, specifically how epoll and io_uring bypass traditional blocking limitations.",

parsed.goal: Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.

modules.title: The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy
modules.description: Before designing distributed systems, you must understand the immutable hardware constraints of a single machine. This module grounds you in the latency numbers that dictate all higher-level software design.

plan.goal: Mathematically quantify system constraints using hardware latency numbers and identify the primary bottleneck in any given system architecture.
concepts: Disk Seek and Network I/O latency
The Bottleneck Principle (CPU vs Memory vs I/O bound)

modules.title: Concurrency Primitives: Processes, Threads, and Coroutines
modules.description: High-level abstractions often mask the immense cost of context switching. Here, you will unearth exactly how the OS kernel manages execution and why modern high-concurrency systems rely on user-space threads.

plan.goal: Explain kernel vs. user-space transitions and justify the use of thread pools and green threads in high-throughput environments.
concepts: OS Kernel vs. User space transitions
OS Threads vs. User-space Threads (Goroutines/Green threads)
prerequisites: The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy

modules.title: Storage Hardware: Disk I/O Mechanics
modules.description: Software design must bow to physical hardware limits. Understanding how SSDs and HDDs actually write data is crucial for understanding why certain databases exist.

plan.goal: Analyze the difference between random and sequential writes at the hardware level and explain how this impacts software data structures.
concepts: HDD Platter Mechanics vs. SSD Flash Memory
sourceRefs: The Google File System (GFS) - Paper Introduction
prerequisites: The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy

modules.title: High-Performance I/O Models
modules.description: This is the engine of servers like Nginx and Redis. We break down how an operating system can handle millions of concurrent connections without exploding thread counts.

plan.goal: Master the mechanics of non-blocking and asynchronous I/O, specifically how epoll and io_uring bypass traditional blocking limitations.
prerequisites: Concurrency Primitives: Processes, Threads, and Coroutines

modules.title: Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals
modules.description: Systems are only as strong as the wires connecting them. This module strips away the HTTP abstraction to look at the actual transport protocol behavior that drives network latency.

plan.goal: Deconstruct TCP to calculate connection overhead and explain how flow control and congestion control limit throughput.
concepts: TCP Congestion Control (Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance)
prerequisites: The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy

modules.title: Modern Network Transport: UDP and QUIC
modules.description: TCP's reliability comes at a cost. You will explore why the modern web (HTTP/3) is moving to UDP-based protocols to solve inherent fundamental bottlenecks.

plan.goal: Explain Head-of-Line blocking in TCP and demonstrate how QUIC over UDP resolves it for modern distributed systems.
concepts: Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking in HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2
prerequisites: Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals

modules.title: Serialization and RPC Mechanics
modules.description: How data is actually packaged and sent. We move from text-based formats you know (JSON) to the highly optimized binary formats required for massive internal scale.

plan.goal: Compare data encoding formats at the byte level and design a schema evolution strategy using Protocol Buffers.
concepts: Text vs. Binary Serialization (JSON/XML vs Protobuf/Avro/Thrift)
prerequisites: Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals

modules.title: Storage Engines I: B-Trees and Read Optimization
modules.description: Opening the black box of traditional relational databases. You will learn exactly how a SQL database structures data on disk to make reads fast.

plan.goal: Trace a read query down to the page level in a B-Tree and design optimal clustered and non-clustered indexes.

modules.title: Storage Engines II: LSM-Trees and Write Optimization
modules.description: When B-Trees fail under extreme write loads, modern systems use LSM-Trees. This is the bedrock of databases like Cassandra and RocksDB.

plan.goal: Trace the lifecycle of a write request through an LSM-Tree and explain the trade-off between write amplification and read latency.
sourceRefs: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data (Paper)
prerequisites: Storage Engines I: B-Trees and Read Optimization

modules.title: Transaction Theory: ACID and Isolation Levels
modules.description: Moving past the acronym to the actual math and logic of transactions. You will learn the exact anomalies that can occur when multiple requests hit a database simultaneously.

plan.goal: Mathematically define transaction isolation levels and correctly identify the anomalies (dirty reads, phantom reads) they prevent.
concepts: Concurrency anomalies: Dirty Reads, Non-repeatable Reads, Phantom Reads, Lost Updates
Isolation Levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable
prerequisites: Concurrency Primitives: Processes, Threads, and Coroutines

modules.title: Transaction Implementation: MVCC
modules.description: How do databases allow multiple users to read and write without locking up the entire system? The answer is Multi-Version Concurrency Control.

plan.goal: Explain how MVCC implements lock-free concurrency and diagram the row versioning process during a transaction.
concepts: Garbage collection of old tuple versions
prerequisites: Transaction Theory: ACID and Isolation Levels

modules.title: Distributed Time and Causality
modules.description: In a distributed system, 'Wall Clock Time' is a lie. This module introduces the fundamental problem of distributed state: proving which event happened first.

plan.goal: Disprove the reliability of wall-clock time in distributed systems and establish event causality using logical clocks.
concepts: Partial vs. Total Ordering of events
sourceRefs: Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System (Lamport Paper)
prerequisites: Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals

modules.title: Distributed Consensus: Foundations and Paxos
modules.description: How do 10 machines agree on a single truth over an unreliable network? We tackle the historically notorious Paxos algorithm to understand the theory of consensus.

plan.goal: Define the mathematical problem of distributed agreement, explain split-brain scenarios, and outline the phases of Paxos.

modules.title: Distributed Consensus: Raft Implementation
modules.description: Moving from the theory of Paxos to the modern standard used in Kubernetes and etcd. We break down Raft step-by-step.

plan.goal: Walk through a Raft leader election and log replication scenario, identifying the exact mechanics that prevent data loss.
concepts: Leader Election phases and randomized timeouts
sourceRefs: Raft: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Paper)

modules.title: The CAP and PACELC Theorems
modules.description: The absolute laws of distributed physics. You cannot build a scalable system without formally acknowledging what you are sacrificing.

plan.goal: Evaluate distributed database architectures against the CAP and PACELC theorems, formally identifying their chosen trade-offs.
concepts: Why 'Partition Tolerance' is mandatory, not optional
sourceRefs: DDIA - Chapter 8: The Trouble with Distributed Systems

modules.title: Replication and Quorums
modules.description: When you duplicate data across nodes, how do you know you're reading the latest version? We dive into the math of read/write quorums.

plan.goal: Tune read and write consistency mathematically using the R + W > N formula and design leaderless replication handling.
sourceRefs: Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store (Paper)

modules.title: Partitioning and Consistent Hashing
modules.description: When data is too large for one machine, it must be split. This module covers the algorithmic elegance of consistent hashing that prevents mass data migrations when servers die.

plan.goal: Design a horizontally sharded data layer using consistent hashing and virtual nodes to handle node failures without a cache stampede.
sourceRefs: Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store (Paper)

modules.title: NoSQL Paradigms and Data Modeling
modules.description: Unlearning the 'SQL vs NoSQL' debate. Here you will learn to map data structures mathematically to query access patterns rather than hype.

plan.goal: Select optimal data models based strictly on read/write ratios and relational depth.
prerequisites: Storage Engines II: LSM-Trees and Write Optimization

modules.title: Load Balancing Mechanics
modules.description: Moving to the macro level: how routers and proxies distribute millions of requests across a fleet of servers efficiently.

plan.goal: Design a multi-tiered traffic distribution topology using Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancing appropriately.
concepts: Routing Algorithms (Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash)
Health Checks and Dynamic Fleet Registration
prerequisites: Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals

modules.title: Caching Topologies and Eviction Policies
modules.description: Caching is not just putting Redis in front of a database. We explore the deep architectural tradeoffs of cache consistency and eviction algorithms.

plan.goal: Design robust caching layers that maintain strict consistency with backing stores and survive extreme traffic spikes.
concepts: Cache Aside vs. Write-Through vs. Write-Behind
Thundering Herd problem and Cache Stampedes

modules.title: Asynchronous Processing and Messaging Systems
modules.description: How to decouple a monolith without creating a distributed ball of mud. We focus on the strict guarantees provided by message brokers.

plan.goal: Architect decoupled asynchronous flows using message brokers, properly categorizing delivery guarantees.
concepts: Event Sourcing and CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)

modules.title: Reliability Engineering and Failure Modes
modules.description: A system isn't designed unless it's designed to fail gracefully. Here we implement the safety nets that prevent cascading outages across microservices.

plan.goal: Design self-healing service interactions that prevent cascading failures under degraded network conditions.

modules.title: Rate Limiting and Backpressure
modules.description: Defending the system from being overwhelmed. We dive into the specific algorithms APIs use to choke traffic efficiently.

plan.goal: Implement and compare algorithmic strategies for API rate limiting and service backpressure.
concepts: Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window Counters

modules.title: The Internals of Observability
modules.description: If you can't see it, you don't understand it. We break down the data structures and protocols behind modern monitoring.

plan.goal: Design the instrumentation layer for a distributed architecture covering the three pillars of observability.

modules.title: Capstone Synthesis: Architecting a Netflix-Scale Global Service
modules.description: The culmination of your journey. You will apply every foundational component—from disk I/O to distributed consensus—into a single, cohesive, globally scalable architecture.

plan.goal: Synthesize all curriculum layers to architect an end-to-end global video streaming and ingestion service, mathematically justifying every infrastructure choice.
concepts: Object Storage (S3) vs. Distributed NoSQL (Cassandra) application
Content Delivery Networks (CDN) and Edge Delivery
Failover mechanisms and Circuit Breakers in practice
prerequisites: Storage Engines II: LSM-Trees and Write Optimization

modules.title: The Physics of Compute: Latency and the Memory Hierarchy
Concurrency Primitives: Processes, Threads, and Coroutines
Network Transport Deep Dive: TCP Internals
Modern Network Transport: UDP and QUIC
Storage Engines I: B-Trees and Read Optimization
Storage Engines II: LSM-Trees and Write Optimization
Transaction Theory: ACID and Isolation Levels
Capstone Synthesis: Architecting a Netflix-Scale Global Service
